methodology: Strict no-cheat protocol: news searched per trading day BEFORE verifying exit prices. 20-calendar-day hold rule applied as default exit unless stop triggered. Entry = next-day open approximated from close+0.3% (market impact). Sources: Nasdaq.com, CNBC, SEC 8-K filings, Bloomberg, Axios.
macro_regime_summary: Q3 earnings season peak for Magnificent 7. Key macro backdrop: Trump-Xi summit confirmed (Oct 20 week), US-China trade rhetoric softening, Russia sanction-driven oil spike (+5% Oct 24), government shutdown data blackout creating earnings call focus. S&P 500 at ~6,664 Oct 20. The dominant theme: AI capex verification — markets wanted confirmation Big Tech was converting AI spend into revenue. Week 1 (Oct 20-24): TSLA earnings miss on op income (-40% YoY), IBM strong beat. Week 2 (Oct 27-31): MSFT Azure +40%, GOOGL first $100B quarter (+34% Cloud), META -9% on tax charge despite beat, AMZN +14% on AWS re-acceleration, AAPL iPhone 17 record. NVDA at $180-207 range, benefiting from every AI capex raise announcement.

trades.thesis: GOOGL reports Q3 on Oct 29. Pre-earnings setup: consensus expecting ~16% revenue growth, Google Cloud running at 29% growth last quarter. AI integration into search (AI Overviews) and GCP Vertex AI have been structural tailwinds all year. Market at all-time highs suggests risk-on environment. The stock is cheap relative to peers at ~25x forward PE vs MSFT at 35x. Expectation: beat + Cloud acceleration + capex raise that signals confidence. 9-day pre-earnings run into a likely beat.
trades.catalyst: Q3 2025 earnings Oct 29 after close. Cloud reacceleration thesis. AI search monetization. Trump-China summit de-escalation removing macro overhang.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: GOOGL delivered its first-ever $100B quarter (revenue $102.35B vs $99.89B est), Google Cloud +34% to $15.2B, EPS $3.10 adj vs $2.33 est — massive beat. Stock +5% in AH on Oct 29. Continued to run post-earnings as the 34% Cloud growth and $155B backlog were interpreted as AI ROI proof. By Nov 10 reached $289.71. The market was trading: 'AI capex is converting to revenue, Cloud acceleration beats expectations, Google is cheap vs MSFT.'
trades.lesson: Pre-earnings long into a quality beat + raise is one of the highest-conviction setups in earnings season. GOOGL's setup was clean: cheap valuation, identifiable catalyst, Cloud reacceleration as the key variable to watch. The actual beat was larger than expected (34% Cloud vs ~29% prior). Hold through earnings was the right call — AH +5% would have been missed if sold pre-earnings. Target was too conservative; stock ran to $290+ in 20 days.

trades.thesis: TSLA reports Q3 on Oct 22 after close. Setup for disappointment: Q3 deliveries of 497K were above expectations (bullish noise absorbed), but operating margin is the key variable — consensus ~7.4%, actual Q3 2024 was 10.8%. Robotaxi fleet at only 500 vehicles is not monetizing. Musk's political role (DOGE/Trump) creates corporate governance discount. Non-GAAP EPS consensus $0.56, but operating income trending toward miss. Risk/reward asymmetric: stock already priced in robotaxi optionality. Short into earnings with stop above recent high.
trades.catalyst: Q3 2025 earnings Oct 22 AMC. Operating margin miss expected. Automotive gross margin pressure from price cuts. Cybercab production not starting until April.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: TSLA reported non-GAAP EPS $0.50 vs $0.56 est, operating income $1.6B vs $1.8B est (-40% YoY). Stock dropped 3-6% in initial reaction Oct 23. However, Musk's robotaxi expansion narrative (fleet to expand rapidly, Cybercab April start) provided a floor. Stock bounced and traded sideways $429-461 range. Market was trading: 'earnings miss on fundamentals, but robotaxi optionality provides support.' The short worked but TSLA's robotaxi story prevented the full drop to $405 target.
trades.lesson: TSLA short on fundamental miss worked but robotaxi narrative is a persistent floor. The real lesson: TSLA shorts need a LARGER thesis than just earnings miss — the optionality narrative absorbs fundamental disappointments. Should have sized smaller (C-grade conviction given Musk's ability to shift narrative). Stop at $462 was correct — stock hit $461 on Oct 28 then came back. The 2.95% gain was acceptable but thesis didn't fully materialize because robotaxi hype is more durable than expected.

trades.thesis: IBM reports Q3 on Oct 22. Setup: AI book of business growing rapidly (prior quarter at $6B+, expected to accelerate). Red Hat mid-teens growth. Infrastructure business had been weak but recovering. IBM is the 'boring AI play' — enterprise AI deployment through consulting and software, not hype. Stock at $276, not extended. Risk: consulting slowdown. But software + AI bookings are the key drivers. Expected beat driven by AI book of business exceeding $8-9B and software revenue growth.
trades.catalyst: Q3 2025 earnings Oct 22 BMO. AI book of business acceleration. Red Hat growth. Infrastructure recovery.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: IBM Q3 beat strongly: revenue $16.3B up 9% YoY (7% constant currency), software up 10%, infrastructure up 17%, AI book of business exceeded $9.5B — well above the ~$6-7B expected. Raised full-year outlook. Stock gapped from $276 to $301 on Oct 24 (+9%). Continued to hold above $300 through Nov 10. Market was trading: 'IBM's enterprise AI deployment is real and accelerating, not just narrative.' The $9.5B AI book of business was the specific number that moved the stock.
trades.lesson: IBM was the cleanest setup in the week: identifiable beat catalyst (AI book of business), reasonable valuation, not a momentum stock so less crowded short interest risk. The gap from $276 to $301 was the core of the trade. Held to $305 by Nov 10. The lesson: enterprise AI deployment plays (IBM, PLTR, SNOW) often have more identifiable catalysts than hyperscalers because the AI book of business metric is trackable quarter-to-quarter. Should have held longer — IBM continued to base above $300.

trades.thesis: Post-TSLA earnings, market has confirmed AI capex cycle is intact (TSLA raised its own capex to $20B+). IBM's AI book of business at $9.5B shows enterprise AI adoption accelerating. NVDA doesn't report until Nov 20, but every AI capex raise by a hyperscaler is incremental demand for Blackwell GPUs. NVDA at $182 after dipping from its October high — risk/reward is entry into MSFT/GOOGL/META/AMZN earnings week which will all contain AI capex guidance. If any of the four hyperscalers raises capex (consensus expects all four to), NVDA reprices higher. Setup: long NVDA into the hyperscaler earnings gauntlet (Oct 27-30).
trades.catalyst: MSFT earnings Oct 29 (Azure guidance), GOOGL Oct 29 (Cloud growth), META Oct 29 (capex raise expected), AMZN Oct 30 (AWS acceleration). Any of these raise AI capex = direct NVDA demand signal.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: NVDA ran from $182 on Oct 23 to $207 by Oct 29 as MSFT (Azure +40%), GOOGL ($91-93B capex), META (raised 2026 capex to $125-145B), and AMZN ($125B 2025 capex) all confirmed massive AI infrastructure spend. NVDA reached its peak for the period. However, META's -9% stock reaction on tax charge created a sell-the-news dynamic and NVDA retreated from $207 peak. By Nov 12 settled at $193. Market was trading: 'capex raises are bullish for NVDA, but META's stock reaction created uncertainty about whether AI spend is generating returns.'
trades.lesson: The capex raise trade worked mechanically — NVDA went from $182 to $207 (+13.7% in 6 days) on the hyperscaler earnings. The mistake was not taking profit at the peak ($207 on Oct 29). Held to Nov 12 at $193 for only 6.4% gain. Lesson: When the catalyst event (earnings week) is over and the key variable (capex raises) has been confirmed, take at least 50% profit. The NVDA trade had a clear catalyst window (Oct 27-31) and should have been sized down after that window closed. Stop was appropriately wide given NVDA's beta.

trades.thesis: META pre-earnings: stock at $749 approaching earnings Oct 29 AMC. Consensus expects strong revenue (~$47B), advertising business resilient, AI features (Meta AI, Llama) driving engagement. Biggest question is capex guidance for 2026. Market expecting ~$60-65B 2025 capex. Setup: META has beaten 8 of last 8 quarters. The stock had been building toward earnings with positive momentum. Enter 2 days pre-earnings to capture the final setup.
trades.catalyst: Q3 2025 earnings Oct 29 AMC. Revenue beat expected. AI engagement metrics. 2026 capex guidance the key variable.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: META beat on revenue ($51.2B, +26% YoY) but a one-time ~$16B non-cash tax charge from Trump's 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' caused net income to plunge 83% ($15.7B → $2.7B). Stock dropped 9% on Oct 30. Then META raised 2026 capex to $125-145B — the market read this as 'AI spend is accelerating faster than ROI,' compounding the selling. Stock fell from $750 to $608 by Nov 14 (a 19% decline). The market was trading: 'tax charge uncertainty + aggressive capex raise without near-term ROI proof = multiple compression.' The key error: didn't know about the tax law change risk.
trades.lesson: This loss was primarily driven by an idiosyncratic risk (one-time tax charge from new legislation) that was not foreseeable from public information before earnings. However, the lesson is: even in a 'strong' earnings setup, position sizing near earnings should be maximum C-grade (8%) because earnings are binary events. The second lesson: when a stock drops 9% immediately post-earnings, that is a D-classification event — should have sold the next morning rather than holding. Held through Nov 14 for -18.8%. Rule violation: did not immediately cut on the -9% earnings reaction; should have applied the stop at $700 strictly.

trades.thesis: AMZN pre-earnings: stock at $227. AWS growth has been reaccelerating (from 17% in Q1 to 19% in Q2 2025). AMZN is the most undervalued of the hyperscalers on an EV/EBITDA basis. AWS backlog growing, new AI deals being signed. The market has been giving AMZN 'wait and see' valuation premium despite MSFT/GOOGL getting re-rated higher on Cloud. Thesis: AWS reports Q3 with 20%+ growth (acceleration from 19% Q2), retail operating margins improve, and the stock closes the valuation gap. Oct 30 after close.
trades.catalyst: Q3 2025 earnings Oct 30 AMC. AWS growth reacceleration to 20%+. Retail operating margin improvement. AI infrastructure investment confirmation.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: AMZN delivered exceptional Q3: AWS revenue $33B (+20% YoY — highest growth in 11 quarters), net sales $180.2B (+13% YoY), stock jumped 14% on Oct 31. AWS backlog grew to $200B. Amazon raised 2025 capex to $125B. Stock went from $227 to $254 peak. However, after the initial surge, the market pulled back as META's capex raise concern spilled over — AMZN's $125B capex raise was also seen as 'spending without near-term returns.' By Nov 14 stock settled at $234. The exit at $234 was below the initial euphoria peak of $254. Market was trading the broader concern about AI capex ROI after the initial reaction.
trades.lesson: AMZN was correct on thesis (AWS reaccelerated to 20%, highest in 11 quarters) but the exit strategy was wrong. The +14% move happened the morning of Oct 31 — should have taken 70-80% profit that morning and held a small residual. Instead, watched the stock run from $227 to $254 peak and then give back most of the gains to $234 by Nov 14. Key lesson: for earnings plays where the catalyst materializes fully (AWS beat, 14% gap up), take most of the profit immediately. The 3.4% gain was far below the 12% achievable if sold on Oct 31 open.

trades.thesis: AAPL at $257 with earnings Oct 30 AMC. Q4 FY2025 (Sep quarter) will capture iPhone 17 launch cycle. iPhone 17 shipped Sep 2025 — first full quarter of iPhone 17 will be Dec quarter, but Sep quarter captures pre-orders and launch week. Services revenue growth sustained above 15%. Tim Cook pre-announced strong iPhone reception. AAPL at $4T market cap milestone is becoming a narrative catalyst. The stock tends to grind higher pre-earnings with low vol. Target: $275+ on Services beat + strong iPhone guidance.
trades.catalyst: Q4 FY2025 earnings Oct 30 AMC. iPhone 17 launch reception. Services revenue growth. Strong Dec quarter guidance expected on iPhone 17 upgrade cycle.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: AAPL reported Q4 FY2025 on Oct 30: revenue $102.5B (+8% YoY), Services $24.97B (+15% YoY), iPhone record revenue for Sep quarter, Dec quarter guidance of $137.97B (vs $132.31B estimate, +4.3% beat). EPS $1.85 vs $1.77 estimate. Stock moved from $270 to $269 on Oct 31 — modest reaction because it was already trading elevated. Continued to grind to $274 by Nov 11. Market was trading: 'iPhone 17 cycle intact, Services growing reliably, guidance conservative as usual.' The stock benefited from the general large-cap tech sentiment but AAPL's reaction was muted relative to AMZN/GOOGL because there was no AI capex surprise.
trades.lesson: AAPL is the most predictable of the Mag7 — beats consistently, guidance tends to be conservative, stock grinds higher. The 6.6% gain in 20 days is representative. The key insight: AAPL's earnings reaction is never dramatic because there are rarely surprises — the stock is a grinder, not a gap-mover. Should hold AAPL pre-earnings as a baseline position, then deploy more risk capital into GOOGL/AMZN where the surprise potential is higher. The Services beat (+15% growth) was the key variable that kept the stock bid.

trades.thesis: US imposed new sanctions on Russian energy giants (Rosneft, Lukoil) on Oct 24, causing Brent crude to surge 5.4% to $65.99. Energy stocks lagged the crude move — XLE only up ~1% while crude was up 5%. This lag creates a catch-up trade. Energy stocks typically reprice crude in 2-3 days. XLE at $43.38. Additionally, Trump-Xi summit news (US-China trade de-escalation) reduces macro headwinds for energy. Short-term energy sector momentum play into the crude shock.
trades.catalyst: US-Russia crude sanctions (Oct 24). Brent crude +5.4% on Oct 24. Trump-Xi summit reducing geopolitical risk. Energy sector underperforming crude move = catch-up lag trade.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: XLE failed to catch up to crude's move as expected. Crude's 5% spike proved transient — markets quickly realized Russia sanctions rhetoric was partially priced in and Brent pulled back from $66 toward $63 in subsequent weeks. XLE traded in the $43-45 range but never broke out. By Nov 13 at $44.57. The market was trading: 'sanctions are marginal, not structural — Russia still finding buyers (India, China), global supply is not meaningfully constrained.' The energy-crude catch-up thesis didn't work because the crude move itself reversed.
trades.lesson: Energy sector catch-up trades on geopolitical crude spikes are often fakeouts — the commodity move itself can be transient if the supply disruption is not structural. Russia sanctions had been an ongoing theme for 3 years; marginal new sanctions don't create lasting supply disruption. Better framework: only play energy if crude sustains above the shock level for 3+ days. The 2.74% gain on XLE was modest — the sector never repriced crude meaningfully. Position sizing was appropriate (small) given this was a momentum setup without fundamental thesis.

best_trade.summary: Pre-earnings long into GOOGL's first-ever $100B quarter. Cloud +34% and EPS $3.10 vs $2.33 estimate was a massive beat. Clean setup: cheap valuation vs peers, clear catalyst, Cloud reacceleration as identifiable variable. Held from Oct 20 to Nov 10, capturing both the pre-earnings drift and the post-earnings continuation.

worst_trade.summary: META pre-earnings long destroyed by an unforeseen one-time $16B tax charge from Trump's 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act,' causing net income to drop 83% despite revenue beating. Compounded by aggressive 2026 capex raise ($125-145B) that signaled AI spend acceleration without near-term ROI. The loss was partly idiosyncratic (tax law change) but also a position sizing error — earnings plays should be C-grade conviction (max 8%) regardless of setup quality. Critical mistake: did not cut when META dropped 9% on Oct 30 open.

period_summary.note_on_borderline: Trades 2, 4, 6, 8 were wins but underperformed thesis — all had the right direction but timing on exit was too late or too early relative to the actual catalyst-driven peak.

key_period_observations: AI capex raises were the single most important variable for all Mag7 stocks in this period. Every company that raised capex guidance saw the stock initially react positively (MSFT, GOOGL, AMZN), but META's +19% loss showed that capex raises without demonstrated ROI can also be punished.
GOOGL was the best risk-reward setup in the period: cheapest valuation, largest absolute beat, Cloud acceleration quantifiable. Should have sized GOOGL as an A-grade position (25% allocation).

META's -18.8% loss illustrates the critical rule: no single earnings trade should be sized above C-grade (8%) because of binary risk. Even a setup with 8 consecutive beats can have a catastrophic idiosyncratic event (new tax legislation).

NVDA trade logic was correct (long into hyperscaler earnings capex gauntlet) but failed to take profits when the catalyst window closed on Oct 31. Exited at $193 vs peak of $207 — left 7% on the table.

The AMZN setup was the most asymmetric: AWS was the laggard among Cloud providers, had been re-accelerating, and was cheapest on valuation. The 14% earnings gap was predictable in direction but magnitude exceeded expectations.

Boeing (BA) would have been an interesting short during this period — strike continuing into 3rd month, deliveries delayed, Congress pressure — but did not pull the trigger due to time constraints on the backtest.

IBM was the stealth winner: enterprise AI deployment (AI book of business $9.5B) was underappreciated and the stock gapped 9% on earnings. Enterprise AI plays with measurable AI book of business metrics are trackable catalysts.
rules_violated: META: Should have cut immediately when stock dropped 9% on Oct 30 (stop was $700, stock went to $665 = stop triggered). Instead held to $608. This is a clear stop-loss violation.
NVDA: Should have taken 50%+ profit on Oct 30-31 when the catalyst window (hyperscaler earnings) closed. Held past the catalyst window for diminishing returns.
AMZN: Should have sold 70-80% on Oct 31 open (+14% gap) when AWS beat was fully priced in. Held to Nov 14 for only 3.4% vs potential 12%.
